channel.title: Algorithms on Megha Bose
channel.description: Recent content in Algorithms on Megha Bose

item.title: Big-O - Why the Right Algorithm Beats Faster Hardware
item.description: Helpful context: How to Think Like a Programmer - Breaking Problems Into Pieces That Yield In 2002, Google engineers faced a problem: they had crawled roughly 2 billion web pages and needed to sort links between them. The naive approach - compare every pair of pages - would require around $4 \times 10^{18}$ comparisons. On the hardware of the day, that would take thousands of years.

item.title: Context-Free Grammars - Languages With Recursive Rules
item.description: Helpful context: Automata Theory - What Machines Can and Cannot Compute Every programming language you’ve ever used is defined by a context-free grammar. When Python tells you SyntaxError: invalid syntax, a parser running a CFG found that your code doesn’t match the grammar. When your browser renders HTML, it’s parsing a context-free structure. When a compiler figures out that 2 + 3 * 4 means 2 + (3 * 4) and not (2 + 3) * 4, it’s using the precedence hierarchy baked into the grammar.

item.title: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy
item.description: Helpful context: Big-O - Why the Right Algorithm Beats Faster Hardware In 1969, the Apollo 11 guidance computer had 4 kilobytes of RAM. Every decision about where to store data was consequential - engineers argued over individual bytes. A wrong choice could mean a program too slow to navigate a lunar approach. Today your phone has roughly 6 billion times more memory. And yet the question hasn’t gone away.

item.title: How Compilers Work - From Source Code to Something a CPU Understands
item.description: Helpful context: Context-Free Grammars - Languages With Recursive Rules Automata Theory - What Machines Can and Cannot Compute You write x = a + b * 2. The compiler turns this into machine code that: Evaluates b * 2 before a + b * 2 because multiplication has higher precedence Computes b * 2 as b + b using a lea instruction instead of a multiply, because addition is faster Eliminates the variable x entirely if it’s never used Inlines the function call if you wrapped this in a tiny function How does a text transformation tool - which is all a compiler is - produce code that is faster than most programmers could hand-write?

item.title: Sorting Algorithms - How Order Turns Hard Problems Easy
item.description: Helpful context: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy In 1945, the US Navy had a problem. They needed to sort 10 million punched cards containing personnel records - and their best method was slow, error-prone, and required enormous physical floor space. John von Neumann, then at the Institute for Advanced Study, thought carefully about the problem and invented merge sort. He didn’t invent it to solve a puzzle.

item.title: Turing Machines - The Minimal Model of Everything a Computer Can Do
item.description: Helpful context: Automata Theory - What Machines Can and Cannot Compute Context-Free Grammars - Languages With Recursive Rules In 1936, a 24-year-old Alan Turing was trying to solve a foundational problem in mathematics. David Hilbert had asked in 1928: is there a mechanical procedure that could, given any mathematical statement, decide whether it is provable? The Entscheidungsproblem - the decision problem. Could mathematics itself be automated?

item.title: Decidability & The Halting Problem - Questions No Program Can Answer
Trees & Heaps - When Flat Structures Hit Their Limit
item.description: Helpful context: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy Every file system on your computer is a tree. Every DOM in a web browser - the structure that defines what a webpage looks like - is a tree. The decision trees inside chess engines are trees. Phylogenetic trees in biology show evolutionary relationships. Organizational hierarchies are trees. Trees are not an abstract data structure invented by computer scientists.

item.title: P, NP, and the Hardest Problems - The Question That Keeps Theorists Up at Night
item.description: Helpful context: Turing Machines - The Minimal Model of Everything a Computer Can Do Decidability & The Halting Problem - Questions No Program Can Answer P vs NP is one of the seven Millennium Prize Problems - a list assembled by the Clay Mathematics Institute in the year 2000, each carrying a one-million-dollar prize for a solution. Of the seven, six remain unsolved. Personally, I suspect anyone capable of actually solving this wouldn’t be doing it for the million dollars - the kind of conviction it takes to crack a problem this deep tends to be its own reward.

item.title: Searching Algorithms - Twenty Steps Through a Million Entries
item.description: Helpful context: Sorting Algorithms - How Order Turns Hard Problems Easy Trees & Heaps - When Flat Structures Hit Their Limit A phone book has 1 million entries. You want to find a name. Linear search: start at page one, read every name until you find it. In the worst case, 1 million comparisons. Binary search: open to the middle. If the name you want comes before the middle, discard the second half.

item.title: Amortized Analysis - The True Cost of a Sequence
item.description: Helpful context: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy A dynamic array doubles its capacity when full. You’re appending elements one by one. Most appends are instant - you write one value into the next slot and you’re done. But every so often, the array is full and you have to allocate a new, larger block of memory and copy everything over. If the array has $n$ elements, that copying costs $O(n)$.

item.title: NP-Completeness - Why Some Problems Are All the Same Problem
item.description: Helpful context: P, NP, and the Hardest Problems - The Question That Keeps Theorists Up at Night In 1971, Stephen Cook proved that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete. One year later, Richard Karp published a landmark paper showing that 21 natural combinatorial problems - scheduling, graph coloring, packing, covering, partitioning - are all NP-complete, by reducing each one to SAT or to each other. The conclusion was striking: these problems, which had been studied for decades in operations research, combinatorics, and computer science, were all interconnected in a deep way.

item.title: Divide & Conquer - Small Savings That Compound
item.description: Helpful context: Sorting Algorithms - How Order Turns Hard Problems Easy Amortized Analysis - The True Cost of a Sequence In 1969, a mathematician named Volker Strassen published a paper that surprised the entire field of numerical computing. For decades, everyone assumed the only way to multiply two $n \times n$ matrices was to compute $n^3$ scalar products - three nested loops. Strassen showed you could do it with $n^{2.

item.title: Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back
item.description: Helpful context: Divide & Conquer - Small Savings That Compound In 1956, a computer scientist named Joseph Kruskal was thinking about how to wire up a network of cities with the minimum total length of cable. He described his algorithm in one sentence: always add the cheapest edge that doesn’t create a cycle. That’s it. No lookahead. No backtracking. No global view of the emerging solution. Just a local rule, applied repeatedly.

item.title: SAT - The Problem at the Heart of NP
item.description: Helpful context: NP-Completeness - Why Some Problems Are All the Same Problem Constraint Satisfaction - Searching Smart, Not Exhaustively In 2016, mathematicians Marijn Heule, Oliver Kullmann, and Victor Marek solved the Boolean Pythagorean triples problem using a SAT solver. The problem: can you color every positive integer either red or blue such that no Pythagorean triple $(a, b, c)$ with $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ is monochromatic (all the same color)?

item.title: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice
item.description: Helpful context: Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back Divide & Conquer - Small Savings That Compound In 1950, Richard Bellman was a mathematician at the RAND Corporation, and he had a problem. He was doing research on multistage decision processes - essentially, how to make a sequence of optimal choices - but the US military was funding his work. The word “mathematical” in a project title, Bellman later wrote, would have “red-flagged” the research.

item.title: Constraint Satisfaction - Searching Smart, Not Exhaustively
item.description: Helpful context: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order A standard 9×9 Sudoku grid has 81 cells. Each cell can hold a digit from 1 to 9. A brute-force solver that tries every possible assignment would explore $9^{81} \approx 2 \times 10^{77}$ grids. For reference, the observable universe contains about $10^{80}$ atoms. You would run out of universe before you ran out of grids to check.

item.title: Space Complexity - When Memory, Not Time, Is the Bottleneck
item.description: Helpful context: P, NP, and the Hardest Problems - The Question That Keeps Theorists Up at Night Turing Machines - The Minimal Model of Everything a Computer Can Do Sorting $n$ numbers takes $O(n \log n)$ time. But how much memory does it need? Merge sort needs $O(n)$ extra space - it copies half the array at each merge step. Heapsort needs $O(1)$ - it works entirely in place.

item.title: Randomized Algorithms - When a Coin Flip Beats Careful Thinking
item.description: Helpful context: Constraint Satisfaction - Searching Smart, Not Exhaustively In 1977, Robert Solovay and Volker Strassen discovered something unsettling: you can check whether a 100-digit number is prime in milliseconds, but the answer is only correct with probability $1 - 2^{-100}$. You could be wrong. But think about what $2^{-100}$ actually means. A 100-bit number requires a specific sequence of 100 coin flips to be wrong. The probability that a cosmic ray flipped a bit in your computer’s RAM during the computation is substantially higher.

item.title: Branch and Bound - Exhaustive Search, Intelligently Pruned
item.description: Helpful context: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Constraint Satisfaction - Searching Smart, Not Exhaustively An airline has 200 planes and 5,000 possible routes. Each plane-route assignment has a fuel cost. Which assignment minimizes total fuel cost? This is an integer programming problem - you can’t assign 0.7 of a plane to a route. Brute force is impossible: $5000^{200}$ possibilities. Continuous relaxation (pretend fractions are allowed) gives you a fast answer, but the answer might require half a plane on one route and 1.

item.title: Complexity Classes - Sorting Problems by How Hard They Are
item.description: Helpful context: Space Complexity - When Memory, Not Time, Is the Bottleneck P, NP, and the Hardest Problems - The Question That Keeps Theorists Up at Night Computer scientists have catalogued hundreds of complexity classes. P, NP, coNP, BPP, ZPP, RP, #P, PP, PSPACE, EXPTIME, PH, IP, PPAD - the list keeps growing. This isn’t taxonomy for its own sake. Each class captures a different kind of resource or computational capability.

item.title: Polynomial Identity Testing - When Randomness Checks Your Algebra
item.description: Helpful context: Randomized Algorithms - When a Coin Flip Beats Careful Thinking Abstract Algebra: Rings & Fields - When Numbers Break Their Own Rules The Schwartz-Zippel lemma is a two-line theorem that powers an entire field. Given two polynomials $f$ and $g$, are they identical? The naive approach - expand both and compare coefficients - can be catastrophically expensive when the polynomials arise from arithmetic circuits with exponentially many terms.

item.title: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order
item.description: Helpful context: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice In 1735, Leonhard Euler was in Königsberg, a city in Prussia built on two islands where the Pregel River forked. The islands and riverbanks were connected by seven bridges. The locals had a recreational puzzle that had never been solved: could you take a walk through the city crossing each of the seven bridges exactly once?

item.title: Graph Coloring - Assigning Colors So No Two Neighbors Agree
item.description: Helpful context: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order NP-Completeness - Why Some Problems Are All the Same Problem In 1852, Francis Guthrie tried to color a map of England’s counties so that no two adjacent counties shared the same color. He noticed four colors always seemed to be enough - no matter how complicated the map, he couldn’t find a counterexample requiring five.

item.title: Minimum Spanning Trees & Union-Find - Spanning a World on a Budget
item.description: Helpful context: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back In the 1920s, Czech mathematician Otakar Borůvka was working for an electrical utility company in Moravia. His job: design an electrical network to connect a set of cities using the minimum amount of cable. He invented an algorithm to do it. Three decades later, in 1956, Joseph Kruskal independently published the same algorithm.

item.title: Shortest Paths - When Greed Works and When It Doesn't
item.description: Helpful context: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice In 1956, Edsger Dijkstra was on a coffee date with his fiancée in Amsterdam. He had no pen or paper. Sitting at a café table, he worked out an algorithm in his head for finding the shortest path between two cities in a road network.

item.title: Network Flow & Matching - The Max, the Min, and Why They're Equal
item.description: Helpful context: Shortest Paths - When Greed Works and When It Doesn’t Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back During World War II, the Soviet Union moved supplies - ammunition, food, fuel - along railroad lines from central cities to the front. US military planners, thinking about how to disrupt the supply chain, wanted to answer a precise question: what is the maximum tonnage of material that can move from the interior to the front per day?

item.title: Graph Algorithm Applicability - Choosing the Right Tool for the Graph
item.description: Helpful context: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order Minimum Spanning Trees & Union-Find - Spanning a World on a Budget Shortest Paths - When Greed Works and When It Doesn’t Network Flow & Matching - The Max, the Min, and Why They’re Equal We’ve covered a lot of graph algorithms at this point. BFS, DFS, topological sort, Kosaraju’s, Kruskal’s, Prim’s, Dijkstra’s, Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, max flow - the list is long.

item.title: PageRank & Power Iteration - Importance as a Fixed Point of a Random Walk
item.description: Helpful context: Graph Algorithms & Traversal - Maps, Networks, and Hidden Order Eigenvalues & Eigenvectors - The Directions a Transformation Leaves Unchanged Markov Chains - Where You’re Going Depends Only on Where You Are In 1998, two Stanford PhD students figured out that the web’s link structure could rank pages by importance. They named their algorithm after one of them: Larry Page. It made them billionaires and changed the internet.

item.title: String Algorithms & Pattern Matching - Finding Needles in Haystacks, Fast
item.description: Helpful context: Data Structures - Storage Is Strategy Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Ctrl+F. Every time you search a document, your computer scans millions of characters looking for a pattern. The naive algorithm is $O(nm)$ - for a 1 MB document and a 10-character query, that’s 10 million comparisons. The KMP algorithm does it in $O(n + m)$. The whole improvement comes down to one insight: when a match fails, don’t restart from scratch.

item.title: Boyer-Moore - The String Matcher That Skips More Than It Reads
item.description: Helpful context: String Algorithms & Pattern Matching - Finding Needles in Haystacks, Fast Most string search algorithms scan left to right. Boyer-Moore scans right to left within the pattern, which lets it skip large chunks of text. For English text searching typical English patterns, Boyer-Moore does far fewer than $n$ character comparisons - it’s sublinear in practice. When grep is fast, Boyer-Moore is often why. The algorithm is surprisingly counterintuitive: it reads the pattern backwards against the text, and a mismatch is information to be exploited, not just a failure to recover from.

item.title: The Viterbi Algorithm - Finding the Most Probable Hidden Path
item.description: Helpful context: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Conditional Probability - What You Already Know Changes Everything Your phone’s voice recognition doesn’t hear words - it hears pressure waves. To understand speech, it needs to find the most likely sequence of words given the sounds it receives. Hidden Markov Models are the statistical backbone. Viterbi is the algorithm that decodes them. It’s in your phone, GPS systems, computational biology (finding genes), and spell-checkers.

item.title: Fast Fourier Transform - Quadratic to Log-Linear by Symmetry
item.description: Helpful context: Divide & Conquer - Small Savings That Compound In 1965, James Cooley and John Tukey published the FFT. It was later discovered that Gauss had the same algorithm in 1805 but never published it. The FFT reduced a computation from $O(n^2)$ to $O(n \log n)$ - for audio processing of $n = 10^6$ samples, that’s from $10^{12}$ operations to $2 \times 10^7$. A factor of fifty thousand.

item.title: Expectation-Maximization - Alternating Between Guessing and Fitting
item.description: Helpful context: Probability Distributions - The Shapes That Randomness Takes Maximum Likelihood - The Parameters That Make Your Data Most Probable Imagine you have height measurements of 1000 people, but you don’t know their genders. You suspect the data comes from two Gaussians - one for each gender. How do you fit both Gaussians when you don’t know which person belongs to which? You can’t - unless you guess.

item.title: Kalman Filtering - Optimal Estimation When Sensors Lie
item.description: Helpful context: Probability Distributions - The Shapes That Randomness Takes Vectors, Matrices & Linear Systems - The Language of Simultaneous Equations In 1960, Rudolf Kálmán published a paper that enabled the Apollo moon landing. The Apollo navigation computer used Kálmán’s filter to track the spacecraft’s position from noisy sensor data. It works by fusing a prediction (from physics) with a measurement (from sensors), weighting each by its uncertainty.

item.title: Metropolis-Hastings - Sampling From Distributions You Can't Integrate
item.description: Helpful context: Probability Distributions - The Shapes That Randomness Takes Markov Chains - Where You’re Going Depends Only on Where You Are In 1953, scientists at Los Alamos needed to compute multidimensional integrals that appear in statistical mechanics - integrals over spaces with billions of dimensions. Standard numerical integration was hopeless: a grid over 100 dimensions with just 2 points per axis requires $2^{100}$ evaluations. Nicholas Metropolis and colleagues invented a different approach: walk randomly through the space, but bias the walk toward high-probability regions.

item.title: Integer Linear Programming - Optimization When Fractions Are Forbidden
item.description: Helpful context: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Convexity - When Local Minimum Means Global Minimum A hospital needs to schedule 50 nurses across three shifts. The constraints pile up quickly: no nurse works two consecutive night shifts, every shift needs at least 8 nurses, certain nurses can’t be rostered together, some nurses are only available on certain days. Write it all out and you have a system of linear inequalities - which is perfectly fine, linear programs are solvable in polynomial time.

item.title: Approximation Algorithms - Close Enough, Fast Enough
item.description: Helpful context: Integer Linear Programming - Optimization When Fractions Are Forbidden Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back The Traveling Salesman Problem is NP-hard. Assuming P ≠ NP, you cannot find the optimal tour in polynomial time. But here is what you can do: find a tour that is at most 1.5 times optimal, in polynomial time, with a proof. This is Christofides' 1976 algorithm - one of the most elegant results in combinatorial optimization.

item.title: Local Search - Improving One Step at a Time Until You Can't
item.description: Helpful context: Approximation Algorithms - Close Enough, Fast Enough Evolution doesn’t plan ahead. Each mutation is accepted if it improves fitness; rejected if it doesn’t. A million iterations of “take a small step in a good direction” produced the eye, the heart, the brain. Local search is this idea applied to optimization: start somewhere, repeatedly move to a better neighbor, stop when no better neighbor exists.

item.title: Simulated Annealing - Escaping Local Optima by Accepting Bad Moves
item.description: Helpful context: Local Search - Improving One Step at a Time Until You Can’t Metropolis-Hastings - Sampling From Distributions You Can’t Integrate When you cool molten steel too fast, it becomes brittle. The atoms don’t have time to find low-energy configurations - they freeze into whatever disordered arrangement they happen to be in. When you cool it slowly (the process called annealing), the atoms can keep rearranging as the temperature drops, settling eventually into a crystal structure with minimal energy.

item.title: Tabu Search - Memory-Guided Optimization That Forbids Its Own History
item.description: Helpful context: Local Search - Improving One Step at a Time Until You Can’t Local search gets stuck at local optima. Simulated annealing escapes via randomness - accept worse solutions with some probability, then cool. Tabu search escapes via memory. Keep a list of recently visited solutions and forbid revisiting them. This forces exploration beyond local optima - and the memory itself becomes a sophisticated guide for where to search next.

item.title: Huffman Coding - Shorter Codes for More Frequent Symbols
item.description: Helpful context: Entropy & Information Theory - The Mathematics of Surprise Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back Every time you send a file over the internet, open a JPEG, or decompress a ZIP archive, a version of the same idea is at work: some symbols in the data appear far more often than others, and it would be wasteful to give every symbol the same number of bits.

item.title: Genetic Algorithms - Evolution as an Optimization Strategy
item.description: Helpful context: Local Search - Improving One Step at a Time Until You Can’t Randomized Algorithms - When a Coin Flip Beats Careful Thinking In the 1970s, John Holland asked a question: can we solve optimization problems the same way evolution solves problems in nature? Evolution doesn’t improve a single organism. It maintains a population. Better organisms reproduce more, passing their structure to offspring. Mutation introduces random variation.

item.title: Beam Search - The Best Path You Can Afford to Search For
item.description: Helpful context: Dynamic Programming - Never Solve the Same Problem Twice Greedy Algorithms - The Art of Never Looking Back GPT-4 generates text one token at a time. At each step it could choose from 50,000 possible tokens. If you greedily pick the most likely token, you often get repetitive, low-quality text - the model commits to an early choice and can’t recover. If you explore all paths (breadth-first search), the tree grows exponentially - 50,000 possibilities at step 1, $50,000^2$ at step 2, completely impractical.

item.title: BFGS - When First-Order Gradients Aren't Fast Enough
item.description: Helpful context: Gradient Descent - Follow the Slope Until the Ground Levels Off Convex Optimization - The Landscape Where Gradient Descent Always Wins Gradient descent is like descending a mountain with your eyes closed - you feel the slope beneath your feet and step in the steepest downhill direction. Newton’s method opens your eyes: it sees not just the slope but the curvature - whether the valley ahead narrows sharply or widens gradually - and adjusts its step accordingly.

item.title: Grover's Algorithm - Quantum Search in Square-Root Time
item.description: Helpful context: Complexity Classes - Sorting Problems by How Hard They Are Randomized Algorithms - When a Coin Flip Beats Careful Thinking Searching a sorted list of $n$ items takes $O(\log n)$ - binary search is one of the first algorithms you learn. Searching an unsorted list takes $O(n)$ - you have to look at each item in turn. No classical algorithm can do better; the lower bound is tight.

item.title: Shor's Algorithm - Quantum Factoring That Threatens Modern Cryptography
item.description: Helpful context: Grover’s Algorithm - Quantum Search in Square-Root Time RSA encryption secures the internet. When your browser negotiates an HTTPS connection, it uses RSA or a related scheme to establish a shared secret. The security of RSA rests on a single assumption: that factoring large numbers is computationally hard. A 2048-bit RSA number has roughly 620 decimal digits. The best known classical algorithm for factoring it - the General Number Field Sieve - would take longer than the age of the universe on any computer we could build or imagine.

item.title: Model Predictive Control - Optimization With a Rolling Horizon
item.description: Helpful context: Ordinary Differential Equations - Equations Whose Unknown Is a Function Numerical Methods - When Exact Answers Cost Too Much to Compute Model predictive control is a control strategy that solves an optimization problem at every time step. Rather than precomputing a fixed policy and applying it open-loop, MPC uses a model of the system to simulate the future, optimizes over a finite window, executes the first control action, then discards the rest of the solution and repeats from the new state.
